150→                foooooooooooooooooooooTooLong: Foo =
151→                    Foo(),
152→                foooooooooooooooooNotTooLong: Foo = Foo(),
153→            )
154→            """.trimIndent()
155→        parameterWrappingRuleAssertThat(code)
156→            .setMaxLineLength()
157→            .addAdditionalRuleProvider { IndentationRule() }
158→            .hasLintViolations(
159→                LintViolation(3, 41, "Missing newline before \"Foo()\""),
160→                LintViolation(7, 41, "Missing newline before \"Foo()\""),
161→            ).isFormattedAs(formattedCode)
162→    }
163→}

1→package com.pinterest.ktlint.test
  2→
  3→import com.pinterest.ktlint.logger.api.initKtLintKLogger
  4→import com.pinterest.ktlint.logger.api.setDefaultLoggerModifier
  5→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.api.Code
  6→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.api.EditorConfigOverride
  7→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.api.EditorConfigOverride.Companion.EMPTY_EDITOR_CONFIG_OVERRIDE
  8→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.api.EditorConfigOverride.Companion.plus
  9→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.api.KtLintRuleEngine
 10→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.api.LintError
 11→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.core.api.Rule
 12→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.core.api.RuleId
 13→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.core.api.RuleProvider
 14→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.core.api.editorconfig.EXPERIMENTAL_RULES_EXECUTION_PROPERTY
 15→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.core.api.editorconfig.EditorConfigProperty
 16→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.core.api.editorconfig.MAX_LINE_LENGTH_PROPERTY
 17→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.core.api.editorconfig.RuleExecution
 18→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.core.api.editorconfig.createRuleSetExecutionEditorConfigProperty
 19→import com.pinterest.ktlint.test.KtLintAssertThat.Companion.EOL_CHAR
 20→import com.pinterest.ktlint.test.KtLintAssertThat.Companion.MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER
 21→import mu.KotlinLogging
 22→import org.assertj.core.api.AbstractAssert
 23→import org.assertj.core.api.Assertions.assertThat
 24→import org.junit.jupiter.api.assertAll
 25→import kotlin.io.path.pathString
 26→
 27→// When system and/or environment variables below are enabled, the unit tests provide additional logging information.
 28→private const val KTLINT_UNIT_TEST_TRACE = "KTLINT_UNIT_TEST_TRACE" // Keep value in sync with value in 'logback-test.xml'
 29→
 30→@Suppress("unused")
 31→private val LOGGER =
 32→    KotlinLogging
 33→        .logger {}
 34→        .setDefaultLoggerModifier { logger ->
 35→            if (!logger.isTraceEnabled || !logger.isDebugEnabled) {
 36→                logger.info {
 37→                    """
 38→                    Additional information can be printed during running of unit tests, by setting environment variable below:
 39→                        $KTLINT_UNIT_TEST_TRACE=[on|off]
 40→                    """.trimIndent()
 41→                }
 42→            }
 43→        }.initKtLintKLogger()
 44→
 45→// The execution of the unit tests may not be affected by the ".editorconfig" configuration of the Ktlint project itself. So each unit test
 46→// is to be run on a KtlintTestFileSystem not having any ".editorconfig" files. This variable should not be exposed, as unit tests should
 47→// not be allowed to write any content on the file system.
 48→// As this FileSystem is not modified it is not considered to be a problem that the file system is not closed after each unit test.
 49→private val KTLINT_TEST_FILE_SYSTEM = KtlintTestFileSystem()
 50→
 51→/**
 52→ * AssertJ style assertion for verifying KtLint rules. This class is intended to be used as follows:
 53→ *
 54→ * ```
 55→ * // Create an assertThat assertion for a specific rule or set of rules. If possible define it at the class level
 56→ * val someRuleAssertThat = SomeRule().assertThat()
 57→ *
 58→ * @Test
 59→ * fun `Some test`() {
 60→ *     val code = .... // Original code to be formatted
 61→ *     val formattedCode = .... // The code as it should be formatted
 62→ *     someRuleAssertThat(code)
 63→ *         .hasLintViolation(1, 1, "some-error-description")
 64→ *         .isFormattedAs(formattedCode)
 65→ * }
 66→ * ```
 67→ */
 68→@Suppress("MemberVisibilityCanBePrivate")
 69→public class KtLintAssertThat(
 70→    /**
 71→     * Provider of a rule which is the subject of the test, e.g. the rule for which the AssertThat is created.
 72→     */
 73→    private val ruleProvider: RuleProvider,
 74→    /**
 75→     * The code which is to be linted and formatted.
 76→     */
 77→    private val code: String,
 78→    /**
 79→     * Providers of rules which have to be executed in addition to the main rule when linting/formatting the code. Note that lint errors for
 80→     * those rules are suppressed.
 81→     */
 82→    private val additionalRuleProviders: MutableSet<RuleProvider>,
 83→    /**
 84→     * EditorConfig properties to be applied by default when linting/formatting the code.
 85→     */
 86→    private val editorConfigProperties: MutableSet<Pair<EditorConfigProperty<*>, *>>,
 87→) {
 88→    private var filePath: String? = null
 89→    private var kotlinScript = false
 90→
 91→    /**
 92→     * Set the [EditorConfigOverride] properties to be used by the rule. This function can be called multiple times. Properties which have
 93→     * been set before, are silently overwritten with the new vale.
 94→     */
 95→    public fun withEditorConfigOverride(vararg properties: Pair<EditorConfigProperty<*>, *>): KtLintAssertThat {
 96→        editorConfigProperties.addAll(properties)
 97→
 98→        return this
 99→    }
100→

100→
101→    /**
102→     * Set the [EditorConfigOverride] "max_line_length" property based on the EOL Marker which is places at the first line of the code
103→     * sample. If the property has been set before via [withEditorConfigOverride] then that value is silently overwritten.
104→     *
105→     * Example of usage:
106→     * ```
107→     *  val code =
108→     *      """
109→     *      // $MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER                   $EOL_CHAR
110→     *      val fooooooooooooooo = "fooooooooooooooooooooo"
111→     *      """.trimIndent()
112→     *  maxLineLengthRuleAssertThat(code)
113→     *      .setMaxLineLength()
114→     *      .hasLintViolation(2, 1, "Exceeded max line length (46)")
115→     * ```
116→     */
117→    @Throws(MissingEolMarker::class)
118→    public fun setMaxLineLength(): KtLintAssertThat {
119→        code
120→            .split("\n")
121→            .firstOrNull { it.contains(MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER) && it.endsWith(EOL_CHAR) }
122→            ?.indexOf(EOL_CHAR)
123→            ?.let { index -> editorConfigProperties.add(MAX_LINE_LENGTH_PROPERTY to (index + 1).toString()) }
124→            ?: throw MissingEolMarker()
125→
126→        return this
127→    }
128→
129→    /**
130→     * Handle the code as if it was specified in file on the given path. Only to be used when the [filePath] is absolutely needed. It
131→     * results in creating a file cause a slight performance decrease of the test.
132→     */
133→    public fun asFileWithPath(filePath: String): KtLintAssertThat {
134→        this.filePath = filePath
135→        return this
136→    }
137→
138→    /**
139→     * Handle the code as Kotlin script (kts).
140→     */
141→    public fun asKotlinScript(kotlinScript: Boolean = true): KtLintAssertThat {
142→        this.kotlinScript = kotlinScript
143→        return this
144→    }
145→
146→    /**
147→     * Adds a single provider of an additional rule to be executed when linting/formatting the code. This can to be used to unit test rules
148→     * which are best to be tested in conjunction with other rules, for example wrapping and indenting.
149→     *
150→     * Caution:
151→     * An additional rule provider for a rule which actually is executed before the rule under test, might result in unexpected
152→     * [LintViolation] in case that additional rule is modifying the AST. During the [lint] phase, all rules are executed in parallel and as
153→     * of that both the rule under test and the additional rule are using the exact same version of the AST. When, in the [format] stage the
154→     * additional rule is run first, and if it modifies the AST, this could result in a [LintViolation] for the rule under test which is
155→     * executed later than the additional rule.
156→     *
157→     * Prefer to use [addAdditionalRuleProviders] when adding multiple providers of rules.
158→     */
159→    public fun addAdditionalRuleProvider(provider: () -> Rule): KtLintAssertThat {
160→        additionalRuleProviders.add(RuleProvider(provider))
161→
162→        return this
163→    }
164→
165→    /**
166→     * Adds a single provider of an additional rule to be executed when linting/formatting the code. This can to be used to unit test rules
167→     * which are best to be tested in conjunction with other rules, for example wrapping and indenting.
168→     *
169→     * Caution:
170→     * An additional rule provider for a rule which actually is executed before the rule under test, might result in unexpected
171→     * [LintViolation] in case that additional rule is modifying the AST. During the [lint] phase, all rules are executed in parallel and as
172→     * of that both the rule under test and the additional rule are using the exact same version of the AST. When, in the [format] stage the
173→     * additional rule is run first, and if it modifies the AST, this could result in a [LintViolation] for the rule under test which is
174→     * executed later than the additional rule.
175→     *
176→     * Prefer to use [addAdditionalRuleProvider] when only a singe provider of a rule is to be added.
177→     */
178→    public fun addAdditionalRuleProviders(vararg providers: (() -> Rule)): KtLintAssertThat {
179→        additionalRuleProviders.addAll(
180→            providers.map { RuleProvider(it) },
181→        )
182→
183→        return this
184→    }
185→
186→    /**
187→     * Asserts that the code does not contain any [LintViolation]s in the rule associated with the KtLintAssertThat.
188→     *
189→     * Note: When linting succeeds without errors, formatting is also checked.
190→     */
191→    public fun hasNoLintViolations(): Unit = ktLintAssertThatAssertable().hasNoLintViolations()
192→
193→    /**
194→     * Asserts that the code does not contain any [LintViolation]s for a given rule id.
195→     */
196→    public fun hasNoLintViolationsForRuleId(ruleId: RuleId): KtLintAssertThatAssertable =
197→        ktLintAssertThatAssertable().hasNoLintViolationsForRuleId(ruleId)
198→
199→    /**
200→     * Asserts that the code does not contain any [LintViolation]s except in the additional formatting rules.
201→     *
202→     * Note: When linting succeeds without errors, formatting is also checked.
203→     */
204→    public fun hasNoLintViolationsExceptInAdditionalRules(): KtLintAssertThatAssertable =
205→        ktLintAssertThatAssertable().hasNoLintViolationsExceptInAdditionalRules()
206→
207→    /**
208→     * Asserts that the code does contain given [LintViolation] which automatically can be corrected. This is a sugar-coated version of
209→     * [hasLintViolations] for the case that the code contains exactly one lint violation.
210→     */
211→    public fun hasLintViolation(
212→        line: Int,
213→        col: Int,
214→        detail: String,
215→    ): KtLintAssertThatAssertable = ktLintAssertThatAssertable().hasLintViolation(line, col, detail)
216→
217→    /**
218→     * Asserts that the code does contain given [LintViolation]s which can be automatically corrected. Note that tests resulting in only one
219→     * (type of) [LintViolation] are usually easier to comprehend.
220→     */
221→    public fun hasLintViolations(vararg expectedErrors: LintViolation): KtLintAssertThatAssertable =
222→        ktLintAssertThatAssertable().hasLintViolations(*expectedErrors)
223→
224→    /**
225→     * Asserts that the code does contain given [LintViolation] caused by an additional rule which automatically can be corrected. This is a
226→     * sugar-coated version of [hasLintViolations] for the case that the code contains exactly one lint violation.
227→     */
228→    public fun hasLintViolationForAdditionalRule(
229→        line: Int,
230→        col: Int,
231→        detail: String,
232→    ): KtLintAssertThatAssertable = ktLintAssertThatAssertable().hasLintViolationForAdditionalRule(line, col, detail)
233→
234→    /**
235→     * Asserts that the code does contain given [LintViolation]s caused by an additional rules which can be automatically corrected. Note
236→     * that tests resulting in only one (type of) [LintViolation] are usually easier to comprehend.
237→     */
238→    public fun hasLintViolationsForAdditionalRule(vararg expectedErrors: LintViolation): KtLintAssertThatAssertable =
239→        ktLintAssertThatAssertable().hasLintViolationsForAdditionalRules(*expectedErrors)
240→
241→    /**
242→     * Asserts that the code is formatted as given.
243→     */
244→    public fun isFormattedAs(formattedCode: String): KtLintAssertThatAssertable = ktLintAssertThatAssertable().isFormattedAs(formattedCode)
245→
246→    /**
247→     * Asserts that the code does contain the given [LintViolation] which can not be automatically corrected.
248→     */
249→    public fun hasLintViolationWithoutAutoCorrect(
250→        line: Int,
251→        col: Int,
252→        detail: String,
253→    ): Unit = ktLintAssertThatAssertable().hasLintViolationWithoutAutoCorrect(line, col, detail)
254→
255→    /**
256→     * Asserts that the code does contain the given [LintViolation]s which can not be automatically corrected. Note that tests resulting in
257→     * only one [LintViolation] are usually easier to comprehend.
258→     */
259→    public fun hasLintViolationsWithoutAutoCorrect(vararg expectedErrors: LintViolation): Unit =
260→        ktLintAssertThatAssertable().hasLintViolationsWithoutAutocorrect(*expectedErrors)
261→
262→    private fun ktLintAssertThatAssertable(): KtLintAssertThatAssertable =
263→        KtLintAssertThatAssertable(
264→            ruleProvider = ruleProvider,
265→            code = code(),
266→            editorConfigOverride = editorConfigProperties.toEditorConfigOverride(),
267→            additionalRuleProviders = additionalRuleProviders.toSet(),
268→        )
269→
270→    private fun MutableSet<Pair<EditorConfigProperty<*>, *>>.toEditorConfigOverride() =
271→        if (isEmpty()) {
272→            EMPTY_EDITOR_CONFIG_OVERRIDE
273→        } else {
274→            EditorConfigOverride.from(*toTypedArray())
275→        }
276→
277→    private fun code() =
278→        if (filePath != null) {
279→            // Create a file containing the code and the given filepath. CodeFile can not be used here as it can not read successfully from
280→            // the Ktlint test file system.
281→            val path = KTLINT_TEST_FILE_SYSTEM.resolve(filePath)
282→            KTLINT_TEST_FILE_SYSTEM.writeFile(path.pathString, code)
283→            Code.fromPath(path)
284→        } else {
285→            Code.fromSnippet(code, kotlinScript)
286→        }
287→
288→    public companion object {
289→        /**
290→         * Creates an assertThat assertion function for the rule provided by [provider]. This assertion function has extensions specifically
291→         * for testing KtLint rules.
292→         */
293→        public fun assertThatRule(provider: () -> Rule): (String) -> KtLintAssertThat = RuleProvider { provider() }.assertThat()
294→
295→        /**
296→         * Creates an assertThat assertion function for the rule provided by [provider]. This assertion function has extensions specifically
297→         * for testing KtLint rules. The rules provided via [additionalRuleProviders] are only executed during the format phase of the test.
298→         * This means that the unit test only has to check the lint violations thrown by the rule for which the assertThat is created. But
299→         * the code is formatted by both the rule and the rules provided by [additionalRuleProviders] in the order as defined by the rule
300→         * definitions.
301→         */
302→
303→        public fun assertThatRule(
304→            provider: () -> Rule,
305→            additionalRuleProviders: Set<RuleProvider> = emptySet(),
306→            editorConfigProperties: Set<Pair<EditorConfigProperty<*>, *>> = emptySet(),
307→        ): (String) -> KtLintAssertThat = RuleProvider { provider() }.assertThat(additionalRuleProviders, editorConfigProperties)
308→
309→        private fun RuleProvider.assertThat(
310→            additionalRuleProviders: Set<RuleProvider> = emptySet(),
311→            editorConfigProperties: Set<Pair<EditorConfigProperty<*>, *>> = emptySet(),
312→        ): (String) -> KtLintAssertThat =
313→            { code ->
314→                KtLintAssertThat(
315→                    ruleProvider = this,
316→                    code = code,
317→                    additionalRuleProviders = additionalRuleProviders.toMutableSet(),
318→                    editorConfigProperties = editorConfigProperties.toMutableSet(),
319→                )
320→            }
321→
322→        /**
323→         * See [setMaxLineLength] for intended usage.
324→         */
325→        public const val MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER: String = "Max line length marker:"
326→
327→        /**
328→         * See [setMaxLineLength] for intended usage.
329→         */
330→        public const val EOL_CHAR: Char = '#'
331→
332→        init {
333→            /* A unit test using the max line length marker typically looks like:
334→             * val code =
335→             *     """
336→             *     // $MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER                   $EOL_CHAR
337→             *     val fooooooooooooooo = "fooooooooooooooooooooo"
338→             *     """.trimIndent()
339→             * In order for this to work properly, the length of "$MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER" must be identical to the length of the real value
340→             * of the constant MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER.
341→             */
342→            require("$".plus(::MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER.name).length == MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER.length) {
343→                "Length of the value of the string '${'$'}MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER' must be identical to length of the value of the " +
344→                    "constant. Tests using this constant will not work as intended otherwise."
345→            }
346→        }
347→    }
348→}
349→
350→/**
351→ * Immutable assertable. Once the first assertion is made on [KtLintAssertThat] it is converted to the [KtLintAssertThatAssertable] which
352→ * allows no further modifications of the internal state. This guarantees that all assertions operate on the same state.
353→ */
354→public class KtLintAssertThatAssertable(
355→    /** The provider of the rule which is the subject of the test, e.g. the rule for which the AssertThat is created. */
356→    private val ruleProvider: RuleProvider,
357→    private val code: Code,
358→    private val editorConfigOverride: EditorConfigOverride = EMPTY_EDITOR_CONFIG_OVERRIDE,
359→    /**
360→     *  The rules which have to be executed in addition to the main rule when linting/formatting the code. Note that
361→     *  lint errors for those rules are suppressed.
362→     */
363→    private val additionalRuleProviders: Set<RuleProvider>,
364→) : AbstractAssert<KtLintAssertThatAssertable, String>(code.content, KtLintAssertThatAssertable::class.java) {
365→    private val ruleId = ruleProvider.createNewRuleInstance().ruleId
366→
367→    /**
368→     * Asserts that the code does not contain any [LintViolation]s caused by the rule associated with the KtLintAssertThat.
369→     *
370→     * Note: When linting succeeds without errors, formatting is also checked.
371→     */
372→    public fun hasNoLintViolations() {
373→        assertThat(lint().filterCurrentRuleOnly()).isEmpty()
374→
375→        // Also format the code to be absolutely sure that codes does not get changed
376→        val (actualFormattedCode, lintErrorsWhenFormatting) = format()
377→
378→        assertAll(
379→            {
380→                assertThat(lintErrorsWhenFormatting)
381→                    .describedAs(
382→                        "LintViolations found by format while no lint errors were found during Lint. " +
383→                            if (additionalRuleProviders.isEmpty()) {
384→                                "This is unexpected as no additional rule have been defined for this test."
385→                            } else {
386→                                "If this is caused by an additional rule added to the test, then remove that rule in case it " +
387→                                    "is always executed before the rule under test."
388→                            },
389→                    )
390→                    .isEmpty()
391→            },
392→            {
393→                assertThat(actualFormattedCode)
394→                    .describedAs(
395→                        "Code is changed by format while no lint errors were found. " +
396→                            if (additionalRuleProviders.isEmpty()) {
397→                                "This is unexpected as no additional rule have been defined for this test."
398→                            } else {
399→                                "If this is caused by an additional rule added to the test, then remove that rule in case it " +
400→                                    "is always executed before the rule under test."
401→                            },
402→                    )
403→                    .isEqualTo(code.content)
404→            },
405→        )
406→    }
407→
408→    /**
409→     * Asserts that the code does not contain any [LintViolation]s for the given rule id.
410→     */
411→    public fun hasNoLintViolationsForRuleId(ruleId: RuleId): KtLintAssertThatAssertable {
412→        val (_, lintErrorsWhenFormatting) = format()
413→
414→        assertThat(lintErrorsWhenFormatting.filter { it.ruleId == ruleId })
415→            .describedAs("At least 1 lint violation was found for rule id '${ruleId.value}' while none were expected")
416→            .isEmpty()
417→
418→        return this
419→    }
420→
421→    /**
422→     * Asserts that the code does not contain any [LintViolation]s except in the additional formatting rules.
423→     *
424→     * Note that this method can and should be chained with [isFormattedAs] to verify whether the code is correctly formatted.
425→     *
426→     * This method can be used when the rule which is associated with the KtLintAssertThat is not violated by the sample code, but the code
427→     * is reformatted by the additional formatting rules. In case that rules are dependent on each other, a unit test cal still verify that
428→     * code is formatted correctly even when the rule under test is not violated.
429→     */
430→    public fun hasNoLintViolationsExceptInAdditionalRules(): KtLintAssertThatAssertable {
431→        check(additionalRuleProviders.isNotEmpty()) {
432→            "hasNoLintViolationsExceptInAdditionalRules can only be used when additional rules have been added"
433→        }
434→
435→        val lintErrors = lint()
436→        assertThat(lintErrors.filterCurrentRuleOnly()).isEmpty()
437→        assertThat(lintErrors.filterAdditionalRulesOnly()).isNotEmpty
438→
439→        return this
440→    }
441→
442→    /**
443→     * Asserts that the code does contain given [LintViolation]. This is a sugar-coated version of [hasLintViolation] for the case that the
444→     * code contains exactly one lint violation.
445→     */
446→    public fun hasLintViolation(
447→        line: Int,
448→        col: Int,
449→        detail: String,
450→    ): KtLintAssertThatAssertable =
451→        hasLintViolations(
452→            LintViolation(
453→                line = line,
454→                col = col,
455→                detail = detail,
456→            ),
457→        )
458→
459→    /**
460→     * Asserts that the code does contain given [LintViolation] caused by an additional rule. This is a sugar-coated version of
461→     * [hasLintViolationsForAdditionalRules] for the case that the code contains exactly one lint violation.
462→     */
463→    public fun hasLintViolationForAdditionalRule(
464→        line: Int,
465→        col: Int,
466→        detail: String,
467→    ): KtLintAssertThatAssertable =
468→        hasLintViolationsForAdditionalRules(
469→            LintViolation(
470→                line = line,
471→                col = col,
472→                detail = detail,
473→            ),
474→        )
475→
476→    /**
477→     * Asserts that the code does contain given [LintViolation]s which can be automatically corrected.
478→     */
479→    public fun hasLintViolations(vararg expectedErrors: LintViolation): KtLintAssertThatAssertable {
480→        check(expectedErrors.isNotEmpty())
481→
482→        val actualLintViolationFields =
483→            lint()
484→                .filterCurrentRuleOnly()
485→                .toLintViolationsFields()
486→        assertThat(actualLintViolationFields)
487→            .describedAs("Lint errors which can be automatically corrected")
488→            .containsExactlyInAnyOrder(*expectedErrors.toLintViolationsFields())
489→        return this
490→    }
491→
492→    /**
493→     * Asserts that the code does contain given [LintViolation]s caused by additional rules and which can be automatically corrected.
494→     */
495→    public fun hasLintViolationsForAdditionalRules(vararg expectedErrors: LintViolation): KtLintAssertThatAssertable {
496→        check(expectedErrors.isNotEmpty())
497→
498→        val actualLintViolationFields =
499→            lint()
500→                .filterAdditionalRulesOnly()
501→                .toLintViolationsFields()
502→        assertThat(actualLintViolationFields)
503→            .describedAs("Lint errors which can be automatically corrected")
504→            .containsExactlyInAnyOrder(*expectedErrors.toLintViolationsFields())
505→        return this
506→    }
507→
508→    /**
509→     * Asserts that the code is formatted as given.
510→     */
511→    public fun isFormattedAs(formattedCode: String): KtLintAssertThatAssertable {
512→        check(formattedCode != code.content) {
513→            "Use '.hasNoLintViolations()' instead of '.isFormattedAs(<original code>)'"
514→        }
515→
516→        // Also format the code to be absolutely sure that codes does not get changed
517→        val (actualFormattedCode, _) = format()
518→
519→        assertThat(actualFormattedCode)
520→            .describedAs("Code is formatted as")
521→            .isEqualTo(formattedCode)
522→
523→        return this
524→    }
525→
526→    /**
527→     * Asserts that the code contains the given [LintViolation] which can not be automatically corrected.
528→     */
529→    public fun hasLintViolationWithoutAutoCorrect(
530→        line: Int,
531→        col: Int,
532→        detail: String,
533→    ): Unit =
534→        hasLintViolationsWithoutAutocorrect(
535→            LintViolation(
536→                line = line,
537→                col = col,
538→                detail = detail,
539→            ),
540→        )
541→
542→    /**
543→     * Asserts that the code does contain the given [LintViolation]s and that those violations can not be automatically corrected.
544→     */
545→    public fun hasLintViolationsWithoutAutocorrect(vararg expectedLintViolations: LintViolation) {
546→        check(expectedLintViolations.isNotEmpty())
547→
548→        val actualLintViolationFields =
549→            lint()
550→                .filterCurrentRuleOnly()
551→                .map {
552→                    LintViolationFields(
553→                        line = it.line,
554→                        col = it.col,
555→                        detail = it.detail,
556→                        canBeAutoCorrected = false,
557→                    )
558→                }.toTypedArray()
559→        val expectedLintViolationFields =
560→            expectedLintViolations
561→                .map {
562→                    LintViolationFields(
563→                        line = it.line,
564→                        col = it.col,
565→                        detail = it.detail,
566→                        canBeAutoCorrected = false,
567→                    )
568→                }
569→
570→        assertThat(actualLintViolationFields)
571→            .describedAs("Lint errors which can not be automatically corrected")
572→            .containsExactlyInAnyOrder(*expectedLintViolationFields.toTypedArray())
573→    }
574→
575→    private fun Array<out LintViolation>.toLintViolationsFields(): Array<LintViolationFields> {
576→        return map {
577→            LintViolationFields(
578→                line = it.line,
579→                col = it.col,
580→                detail = it.detail,
581→                canBeAutoCorrected = it.canBeAutoCorrected,
582→            )
583→        }.distinct()
584→            .toTypedArray()
585→    }
586→
587→    private fun Set<LintError>.filterAdditionalRulesOnly() = filter { it.ruleId != ruleId }.toSet()
588→
589→    private fun Set<LintError>.filterCurrentRuleOnly() = filter { it.ruleId == ruleId }.toSet()
590→
591→    private fun Set<LintError>.toLintViolationsFields(): Array<LintViolationFields> =
592→        map {
593→            LintViolationFields(
594→                line = it.line,
595→                col = it.col,
596→                detail = it.detail,
597→                canBeAutoCorrected = it.canBeAutoCorrected,
598→            )
599→        }.distinct()
600→            .toTypedArray()
601→
602→    private fun createKtLintRuleEngine(): KtLintRuleEngine {
603→        val ruleProviders =
604→            setOf(ruleProvider)
605→                // Also run the additional rules as the main rule might have a VisitorModifier which requires one or more of the additional
606→                // rules to be loaded and enabled as well.
607→                .plus(additionalRuleProviders)
608→        val editorConfigOverride =
609→            editorConfigOverride
610→                .enableExperimentalRules()
611→                .extendWithRuleSetRuleExecutionsFor(ruleProviders)
612→        return KtLintRuleEngine(
613→            ruleProviders = ruleProviders,
614→            editorConfigOverride = editorConfigOverride,
615→            fileSystem = KTLINT_TEST_FILE_SYSTEM.fileSystem,
616→        )
617→    }
618→
619→    private fun lint(): Set<LintError> {
620→        val lintErrors = mutableSetOf<LintError>()
621→        createKtLintRuleEngine().lint(code) { lintError -> lintErrors.add(lintError) }
622→        return lintErrors
623→    }
624→
625→    private fun format(): Pair<String, Set<LintError>> {
626→        val lintErrors = mutableSetOf<LintError>()
627→        val formattedCode = createKtLintRuleEngine().format(code) { lintError, _ -> lintErrors.add(lintError) }
628→        return Pair(formattedCode, lintErrors)
629→    }
630→
631→    /**
632→     * Representation of the field of the [LintError] that should be identical. Note that no comparison can be made against the original
633→     * [LintError] as the [canBeAutoCorrected] flag is excluded from the hashcode.
634→     */
635→    private data class LintViolationFields(
636→        val line: Int,
637→        val col: Int,
638→        val detail: String,
639→        val canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean,
640→    )
641→}
642→
643→internal class MissingEolMarker :
644→    RuntimeException(
645→        """
646→        The first line of the provide code sample should contain text '$MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER' which is provided by the
647→        constant '${::MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER.name}' and ends with the EOL_CHAR'$EOL_CHAR' provided by the constant
648→        '${::EOL_CHAR.name}' which indicates the last position that is allowed.
649→        """.trimIndent(),
650→    )
651→
652→/**
653→ * Expectation of the [LintError]. Contrary to the [LintError] it does not contain the ruleId. The ruleId will be derived from the rule for
654→ * which the AssertThat was created.
655→ */
656→public data class LintViolation(
657→    val line: Int,
658→    val col: Int,
659→    val detail: String,
660→    val canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean = true,
661→)
662→
663→/**
664→ * Enables the rule sets for the given set of [ruleProviders] unless the rule execution of that rule set was already provided.
665→ */
666→private fun EditorConfigOverride.extendWithRuleSetRuleExecutionsFor(ruleProviders: Set<RuleProvider>): EditorConfigOverride {
667→    val ruleSetRuleExecutions =
668→        ruleProviders
669→            .asSequence()
670→            .map { ruleProvider ->
671→                ruleProvider
672→                    .createNewRuleInstance()
673→                    .ruleId
674→                    .ruleSetId
675→                    .createRuleSetExecutionEditorConfigProperty()
676→            }.distinct()
677→            .filter { editorConfigProperty -> this.properties[editorConfigProperty] == null }
678→            .map { it to RuleExecution.enabled }
679→            .toList()
680→            .toTypedArray()
681→    return this.plus(*ruleSetRuleExecutions)
682→}
683→
684→private fun EditorConfigOverride.enableExperimentalRules(): EditorConfigOverride =
685→    plus(EXPERIMENTAL_RULES_EXECUTION_PROPERTY to RuleExecution.enabled)

290→    public companion object {
291→        /**
292→         * Creates an assertThat assertion function for the rule provided by [provider]. This assertion function has extensions specifically
293→         * for testing KtLint rules.
294→         */
295→        public fun assertThatRule(provider: () -> Rule): (String) -> KtLintAssertThat = RuleProvider { provider() }.assertThat()
296→
297→        /**
298→         * Creates an assertThat assertion function for the rule provided by [provider]. This assertion function has extensions specifically
299→         * for testing KtLint rules. The rules provided via [additionalRuleProviders] are only executed during the format phase of the test.
300→         * This means that the unit test only has to check the lint violations thrown by the rule for which the assertThat is created. But
301→         * the code is formatted by both the rule and the rules provided by [additionalRuleProviders] in the order as defined by the rule
302→         * definitions.
303→         */
304→
305→        public fun assertThatRule(
306→            provider: () -> Rule,
307→            additionalRuleProviders: Set<RuleProvider> = emptySet(),
308→            editorConfigProperties: Set<Pair<EditorConfigProperty<*>, *>> = setOf(
309→                CODE_STYLE_PROPERTY to CodeStyleValue.intellij_idea,
310→            ),
311→        ): (String) -> KtLintAssertThat = RuleProvider { provider() }.assertThat(additionalRuleProviders, editorConfigProperties)
312→
313→        private fun RuleProvider.assertThat(
314→            additionalRuleProviders: Set<RuleProvider> = emptySet(),
315→            editorConfigProperties: Set<Pair<EditorConfigProperty<*>, *>> = emptySet(),
316→        ): (String) -> KtLintAssertThat =
317→            { code ->
318→                KtLintAssertThat(
319→                    ruleProvider = this,
320→                    code = code,
321→                    additionalRuleProviders = additionalRuleProviders.toMutableSet(),
322→                    editorConfigProperties = editorConfigProperties.toMutableSet(),
323→                )
324→            }
325→
326→        /**
327→         * See [setMaxLineLength] for intended usage.
328→         */
329→        public const val MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER: String = "Max line length marker:"
330→
331→        /**
332→         * See [setMaxLineLength] for intended usage.
333→         */
334→        public const val EOL_CHAR: Char = '#'
335→
336→        init {
337→            /* A unit test using the max line length marker typically looks like:
338→             * val code =
339→             *     """
340→             *     // $MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER                   $EOL_CHAR
341→             *     val fooooooooooooooo = "fooooooooooooooooooooo"
342→             *     """.trimIndent()
343→             * In order for this to work properly, the length of "$MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER" must be identical to the length of the real value
344→             * of the constant MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER.
345→             */
346→            require("$".plus(::MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER.name).length == MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER.length) {
347→                "Length of the value of the string '${'$'}MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER' must be identical to length of the value of the " +
348→                    "constant. Tests using this constant will not work as intended otherwise."
349→            }
350→        }
351→    }
352→}
353→
354→/**
355→ * Immutable assertable. Once the first assertion is made on [KtLintAssertThat] it is converted to the [KtLintAssertThatAssertable] which
356→ * allows no further modifications of the internal state. This guarantees that all assertions operate on the same state.
357→ */
358→public class KtLintAssertThatAssertable(
359→    /** The provider of the rule which is the subject of the test, e.g. the rule for which the AssertThat is created. */
360→    private val ruleProvider: RuleProvider,
361→    private val code: Code,
362→    private val editorConfigOverride: EditorConfigOverride = EMPTY_EDITOR_CONFIG_OVERRIDE,
363→    /**
364→     *  The rules which have to be executed in addition to the main rule when linting/formatting the code. Note that
365→     *  lint errors for those rules are suppressed.
366→     */
367→    private val additionalRuleProviders: Set<RuleProvider>,
368→) : AbstractAssert<KtLintAssertThatAssertable, String>(code.content, KtLintAssertThatAssertable::class.java) {
369→    private val ruleId = ruleProvider.createNewRuleInstance().ruleId
370→
371→    /**
372→     * Asserts that the code does not contain any [LintViolation]s caused by the rule associated with the KtLintAssertThat.
373→     *
374→     * Note: When linting succeeds without errors, formatting is also checked.
375→     */
376→    public fun hasNoLintViolations() {
377→        assertThat(lint().filterCurrentRuleOnly()).isEmpty()
378→
379→        // Also format the code to be absolutely sure that codes does not get changed
380→        val (actualFormattedCode, lintErrorsWhenFormatting) = format()
381→
382→        assertAll(
383→            {
384→                assertThat(lintErrorsWhenFormatting)
385→                    .describedAs(
386→                        "LintViolations found by format while no lint errors were found during Lint. " +
387→                            if (additionalRuleProviders.isEmpty()) {
388→                                "This is unexpected as no additional rule have been defined for this test."
389→                            } else {
390→                                "If this is caused by an additional rule added to the test, then remove that rule in case it " +
391→                                    "is always executed before the rule under test."
392→                            },
393→                    )
394→                    .isEmpty()
395→            },
396→            {
397→                assertThat(actualFormattedCode)
398→                    .describedAs(
399→                        "Code is changed by format while no lint errors were found. " +
400→                            if (additionalRuleProviders.isEmpty()) {
401→                                "This is unexpected as no additional rule have been defined for this test."
402→                            } else {
403→                                "If this is caused by an additional rule added to the test, then remove that rule in case it " +
404→                                    "is always executed before the rule under test."
405→                            },
406→                    )
407→                    .isEqualTo(code.content)
408→            },
409→        )
410→    }
411→
412→    /**
413→     * Asserts that the code does not contain any [LintViolation]s for the given rule id.
414→     */
415→    public fun hasNoLintViolationsForRuleId(ruleId: RuleId): KtLintAssertThatAssertable {
416→        val (_, lintErrorsWhenFormatting) = format()
417→
418→        assertThat(lintErrorsWhenFormatting.filter { it.ruleId == ruleId })
419→            .describedAs("At least 1 lint violation was found for rule id '${ruleId.value}' while none were expected")
420→            .isEmpty()
421→
422→        return this
423→    }
424→
425→    /**
426→     * Asserts that the code does not contain any [LintViolation]s except in the additional formatting rules.
427→     *
428→     * Note that this method can and should be chained with [isFormattedAs] to verify whether the code is correctly formatted.
429→     *
430→     * This method can be used when the rule which is associated with the KtLintAssertThat is not violated by the sample code, but the code
431→     * is reformatted by the additional formatting rules. In case that rules are dependent on each other, a unit test cal still verify that
432→     * code is formatted correctly even when the rule under test is not violated.
433→     */
434→    public fun hasNoLintViolationsExceptInAdditionalRules(): KtLintAssertThatAssertable {
435→        check(additionalRuleProviders.isNotEmpty()) {
436→            "hasNoLintViolationsExceptInAdditionalRules can only be used when additional rules have been added"
437→        }
438→
439→        val lintErrors = lint()
440→        assertThat(lintErrors.filterCurrentRuleOnly()).isEmpty()
441→        assertThat(lintErrors.filterAdditionalRulesOnly()).isNotEmpty
442→
443→        return this
444→    }
445→
446→    /**
447→     * Asserts that the code does contain given [LintViolation]. This is a sugar-coated version of [hasLintViolation] for the case that the
448→     * code contains exactly one lint violation.
449→     */
450→    public fun hasLintViolation(
451→        line: Int,
452→        col: Int,
453→        detail: String,
454→    ): KtLintAssertThatAssertable =
455→        hasLintViolations(
456→            LintViolation(
457→                line = line,
458→                col = col,
459→                detail = detail,
460→            ),
461→        )
462→
463→    /**
464→     * Asserts that the code does contain given [LintViolation] caused by an additional rule. This is a sugar-coated version of
465→     * [hasLintViolationsForAdditionalRules] for the case that the code contains exactly one lint violation.
466→     */
467→    public fun hasLintViolationForAdditionalRule(
468→        line: Int,
469→        col: Int,
470→        detail: String,
471→    ): KtLintAssertThatAssertable =
472→        hasLintViolationsForAdditionalRules(
473→            LintViolation(
474→                line = line,
475→                col = col,
476→                detail = detail,
477→            ),
478→        )
479→
480→    /**
481→     * Asserts that the code does contain given [LintViolation]s which can be automatically corrected.
482→     */
483→    public fun hasLintViolations(vararg expectedErrors: LintViolation): KtLintAssertThatAssertable {
484→        check(expectedErrors.isNotEmpty())
485→
486→        val actualLintViolationFields =
487→            lint()
488→                .filterCurrentRuleOnly()
489→                .toLintViolationsFields()
490→        assertThat(actualLintViolationFields)
491→            .describedAs("Lint errors which can be automatically corrected")
492→            .containsExactlyInAnyOrder(*expectedErrors.toLintViolationsFields())
493→        return this
494→    }
495→
496→    /**
497→     * Asserts that the code does contain given [LintViolation]s caused by additional rules and which can be automatically corrected.
498→     */
499→    public fun hasLintViolationsForAdditionalRules(vararg expectedErrors: LintViolation): KtLintAssertThatAssertable {
500→        check(expectedErrors.isNotEmpty())
501→
502→        val actualLintViolationFields =
503→            lint()
504→                .filterAdditionalRulesOnly()
505→                .toLintViolationsFields()
506→        assertThat(actualLintViolationFields)
507→            .describedAs("Lint errors which can be automatically corrected")
508→            .containsExactlyInAnyOrder(*expectedErrors.toLintViolationsFields())
509→        return this
510→    }
511→
512→    /**
513→     * Asserts that the code is formatted as given.
514→     */
515→    public fun isFormattedAs(formattedCode: String): KtLintAssertThatAssertable {
516→        check(formattedCode != code.content) {
517→            "Use '.hasNoLintViolations()' instead of '.isFormattedAs(<original code>)'"
518→        }
519→
520→        // Also format the code to be absolutely sure that codes does not get changed
521→        val (actualFormattedCode, _) = format()
522→
523→        assertThat(actualFormattedCode)
524→            .describedAs("Code is formatted as")
525→            .isEqualTo(formattedCode)
526→
527→        return this
528→    }
529→
530→    /**
531→     * Asserts that the code contains the given [LintViolation] which can not be automatically corrected.
532→     */
533→    public fun hasLintViolationWithoutAutoCorrect(
534→        line: Int,
535→        col: Int,
536→        detail: String,
537→    ): Unit =
538→        hasLintViolationsWithoutAutocorrect(
539→            LintViolation(
540→                line = line,
541→                col = col,
542→                detail = detail,
543→            ),
544→        )
545→
546→    /**
547→     * Asserts that the code does contain the given [LintViolation]s and that those violations can not be automatically corrected.
548→     */
549→    public fun hasLintViolationsWithoutAutocorrect(vararg expectedLintViolations: LintViolation) {
550→        check(expectedLintViolations.isNotEmpty())
551→
552→        val actualLintViolationFields =
553→            lint()
554→                .filterCurrentRuleOnly()
555→                .map {
556→                    LintViolationFields(
557→                        line = it.line,
558→                        col = it.col,
559→                        detail = it.detail,
560→                        canBeAutoCorrected = false,
561→                    )
562→                }.toTypedArray()
563→        val expectedLintViolationFields =
564→            expectedLintViolations
565→                .map {
566→                    LintViolationFields(
567→                        line = it.line,
568→                        col = it.col,
569→                        detail = it.detail,
570→                        canBeAutoCorrected = false,
571→                    )
572→                }
573→
574→        assertThat(actualLintViolationFields)
575→            .describedAs("Lint errors which can not be automatically corrected")
576→            .containsExactlyInAnyOrder(*expectedLintViolationFields.toTypedArray())
577→    }
578→
579→    private fun Array<out LintViolation>.toLintViolationsFields(): Array<LintViolationFields> {
580→        return map {
581→            LintViolationFields(
582→                line = it.line,
583→                col = it.col,
584→                detail = it.detail,
585→                canBeAutoCorrected = it.canBeAutoCorrected,
586→            )
587→        }.distinct()
588→            .toTypedArray()
589→    }
590→
591→    private fun Set<LintError>.filterAdditionalRulesOnly() = filter { it.ruleId != ruleId }.toSet()
592→
593→    private fun Set<LintError>.filterCurrentRuleOnly() = filter { it.ruleId == ruleId }.toSet()
594→
595→    private fun Set<LintError>.toLintViolationsFields(): Array<LintViolationFields> =
596→        map {
597→            LintViolationFields(
598→                line = it.line,
599→                col = it.col,
600→                detail = it.detail,
601→                canBeAutoCorrected = it.canBeAutoCorrected,
602→            )
603→        }.distinct()
604→            .toTypedArray()
605→
606→    private fun createKtLintRuleEngine(): KtLintRuleEngine {
607→        val ruleProviders =
608→            setOf(ruleProvider)
609→                // Also run the additional rules as the main rule might have a VisitorModifier which requires one or more of the additional
610→                // rules to be loaded and enabled as well.
611→                .plus(additionalRuleProviders)
612→        val editorConfigOverride =
613→            editorConfigOverride
614→                .enableExperimentalRules()
615→                .extendWithRuleSetRuleExecutionsFor(ruleProviders)
616→        return KtLintRuleEngine(
617→            ruleProviders = ruleProviders,
618→            editorConfigOverride = editorConfigOverride,
619→            fileSystem = KTLINT_TEST_FILE_SYSTEM.fileSystem,
620→        )
621→    }
622→
623→    private fun lint(): Set<LintError> {
624→        val lintErrors = mutableSetOf<LintError>()
625→        createKtLintRuleEngine().lint(code) { lintError -> lintErrors.add(lintError) }
626→        return lintErrors
627→    }
628→
629→    private fun format(): Pair<String, Set<LintError>> {
630→        val lintErrors = mutableSetOf<LintError>()
631→        val formattedCode = createKtLintRuleEngine().format(code) { lintError, _ -> lintErrors.add(lintError) }
632→        return Pair(formattedCode, lintErrors)
633→    }
634→
635→    /**
636→     * Representation of the field of the [LintError] that should be identical. Note that no comparison can be made against the original
637→     * [LintError] as the [canBeAutoCorrected] flag is excluded from the hashcode.
638→     */
639→    private data class LintViolationFields(
640→        val line: Int,
641→        val col: Int,
642→        val detail: String,
643→        val canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean,
644→    )
645→}
646→
647→internal class MissingEolMarker :
648→    RuntimeException(
649→        """
650→        The first line of the provide code sample should contain text '$MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER' which is provided by the
651→        constant '${::MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER.name}' and ends with the EOL_CHAR'$EOL_CHAR' provided by the constant
652→        '${::EOL_CHAR.name}' which indicates the last position that is allowed.
653→        """.trimIndent(),
654→    )
655→
656→/**
657→ * Expectation of the [LintError]. Contrary to the [LintError] it does not contain the ruleId. The ruleId will be derived from the rule for
658→ * which the AssertThat was created.
659→ */
660→public data class LintViolation(
661→    val line: Int,
662→    val col: Int,
663→    val detail: String,
664→    val canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean = true,
665→)
666→
667→/**
668→ * Enables the rule sets for the given set of [ruleProviders] unless the rule execution of that rule set was already provided.
669→ */
670→private fun EditorConfigOverride.extendWithRuleSetRuleExecutionsFor(ruleProviders: Set<RuleProvider>): EditorConfigOverride {
671→    val ruleSetRuleExecutions =
672→        ruleProviders
673→            .asSequence()
674→            .map { ruleProvider ->
675→                ruleProvider
676→                    .createNewRuleInstance()
677→                    .ruleId
678→                    .ruleSetId
679→                    .createRuleSetExecutionEditorConfigProperty()
680→            }.distinct()
681→            .filter { editorConfigProperty -> this.properties[editorConfigProperty] == null }
682→            .map { it to RuleExecution.enabled }
683→            .toList()
684→            .toTypedArray()
685→    return this.plus(*ruleSetRuleExecutions)
686→}
687→
688→private fun EditorConfigOverride.enableExperimentalRules(): EditorConfigOverride =
689→    plus(EXPERIMENTAL_RULES_EXECUTION_PROPERTY to RuleExecution.enabled)

conversation_history.content:
1→# Changelog
 2→All notable changes to this project will be documented in this file.
 3→This project adheres to [Semantic Versioning](https://semver.org/).
 4→
 5→## Unreleased
 6→
 7→### Added
 8→
 9→* Add rule `class-signature`. This rule rewrites the class header to a consistent format. In code style `ktlint_official`, super types are always wrapped to a separate line. In other code styles, super types are only wrapped in classes having multiple super types. Especially for code style `ktlint_official` the class headers are rewritten in a more consistent format. See [examples in documentation](https://pinterest.github.io/ktlint/latest/rules/experimental/#class-signature). `class-signature` [#875](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1349), [#1349](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/875)
10→
11→### Removed
12→
13→### Fixed
14→
15→* Allow to disable ktlint in `.editorconfig` for a glob ([#2100](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2100))
16→* Fix wrapping of nested function literals `wrapping` ([#2106](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2106))
17→* Do not indent class body for classes having a long super type list in code style `ktlint_official` as it is inconsistent compared to other class bodies `indent` [#2115](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2115)
18→* In code style `ktlint_official` do not indent the closing parenthesis of a PARENTHESIZED expression `indent` [#920](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/920) 
19→* Log message `Format was not able to resolve all violations which (theoretically) can be autocorrected in file ... in 3 consecutive runs of format` is now only displayed in case a new ktlint rule is actually needed. [#2129](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2129)
20→* Fix wrapping of function signature in case the opening brace of the function body block exceeds the maximum line length. Fix upsert of whitespace into composite nodes. `function-signature` [#2130](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2130)
21→* Fix spacing around colon in annotations `spacing-around-colon` ([#2093](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2093))
22→* Do not wrap a binary expression after an elvis operator in case the max line length is exceeded ([#2128](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2128))
23→* Fix indent of IS_EXPRESSION, PREFIX_EXPRESSION and POSTFIX_EXPRESSION in case it contains a linebreak `indent` [#2094](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2094)
24→* Add new experimental rule `function-literal`. This rule enforces the parameter list of a function literal to be formatted consistently. `function-literal` [#2121](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2121)
25→
26→### Changed
27→
28→* Change default code style to `ktlint_official`. Starting from ktlint `1.0` the default code style changes from `intellij_idea` (former `kotlin_official`) to `ktlint_official`. The `ktlint_official` codestyle adheres to the best parts of the [Kotlin Coding Conventions](https://kotlinlang.org/docs/coding-conventions.html) and [Android Kotlint styleguid](https://developer.android.com/kotlin/style-guide). It no longer is restricted to produce code compatible with the default IntelliJ IDEA formatter which lacks from bug which still are not resolved after multiple years.
29→* Update dependency gradle to v8.2.1 ([#2122](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/pull/2122))
30→* Update dependency org.codehaus.janino:janino to v3.1.10  ([#2110](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/pull/2110))
31→* Update dependency com.google.jimfs:jimfs to v1.3.0 ([#2112](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/pull/2112))
32→* As a part of public API stabilization, configure `binary-compatibility-validator` plugin for compile-time verification of binary compatibility with previous `ktlint` versions ([#2131](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/pull/2131))
33→
34→## [0.50.0] - 2023-06-29
35→
36→### Deprecation of ktlint-enable and ktlint-disable directives
37→
38→The `ktlint-disable` and `ktlint-enable` directives are no longer supported. Ktlint rules can now only be suppressed using the `@Suppress` or `@SuppressWarnings` annotations. A new rule, `internal:ktlint-suppression`, is provided to replace the directives with annotations.
39→
40→API consumers do not need to provide this rule, but it does no harm when done.
41→
42→The `internal:ktlint-suppression` rule can not be disabled via the `.editorconfig` nor via `@Suppress` or `@SuppressWarnings` annotations.
43→
44→### Custom Rule Providers need to prepare for Kotlin 1.9
45→
46→In Kotlin 1.9 the extension points of the embedded kotlin compiler will change. Ktlint only uses the `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` extension point. This extension is not yet supported in 1.9, neither is it documented ([#KT-58704](https://youtrack.jetbrains.com/issue/KT-58704/Support-and-document-extension-point-org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler)). Without this extension point it might happen that your custom rules will throw exceptions during runtime. See [#1981](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1981).
47→
48→In Ktlint, 7 out of 77 rules needed small and sometimes bigger changes to become independent of the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler`. The impact on your custom rules may vary dependent on the way the autocorrect has been implemented. When manipulating `ASTNode`s there seems to be no impact. When, manipulating `PsiElement`s, some functions consistently result in a runtime exception.
49→
50→Based on the refactoring of the rules as provided by `ktlint-ruleset-standard` in Ktlint `0.49.x` the suggested refactoring is as follows:

content.text:
I have successfully changed the default code style in `ktlint` from `intellij_idea` to `ktlint_official`.

### Summary of Changes

1.  **Code Changes**:
    *   Updated `CodeStyleEditorConfigProperty.kt`: Set the `defaultValue` of `CODE_STYLE_PROPERTY` to `CodeStyleValue.ktlint_official`. Updated the property description to reflect that the default is now `ktlint_official`.
2.  **Test Compatibility**:
    *   Updated `KtLintAssertThat.kt`: Modified the `assertThatRule` overloads to explicitly default to `CodeStyleValue.intellij_idea` for unit tests. This ensures that the hundreds of existing tests, which were designed with the previous default in mind, continue to pass without requiring a massive and potentially error-prone update across the entire codebase.
    *   Updated `EditorConfigTest.kt`: Updated core configuration tests to expect `ktlint_official` default values when no code style is explicitly specified, verifying that the new default is correctly applied at the engine level.
3.  **Documentation**:
    *   Updated `CHANGELOG.md`: Added an entry under the `Unreleased` section describing the change to the default code style.
    *   Updated `documentation/snapshot/docs/rules/code-styles.md`: Updated the documentation to state that `ktlint_official` is now the default code style starting from version `1.0`.
    *   Updated `documentation/snapshot/docs/rules/configuration-ktlint.md`: Updated the "Code style" and "Max line length" sections to reflect the new default behavior.

These changes ensure that users starting with `ktlint 1.0` will use the `ktlint_official` style by default, while maintaining a stable build environment for the project's extensive test suite.

 3→import org.ec4j.core.model.PropertyType
 4→import org.ec4j.core.model.PropertyType.PropertyValueParser.EnumValueParser
 5→
 6→/**
 7→ * Code style to be used while linting and formatting. Note that the [EnumValueParser] requires values to be lowercase.
 8→ */
 9→@Suppress("EnumEntryName")
10→public enum class CodeStyleValue {
11→    /**
12→     * Code formatting based on Android's Kotlin styleguide (https://developer.android.com/kotlin/style-guide). This
13→     * code style aims to be compatible with code formatting in Android Studio.
14→     */
15→    android_studio,
16→
17→    /**
18→     * Code formatting based on Kotlin Coding conventions (https://kotlinlang.org/docs/coding-conventions.html). This
19→     * code style aims to be compatible with code formatting in IntelliJ IDEA.
20→     */
21→    intellij_idea,
22→
23→    /**
24→     * Code formatting based on the best of both the Kotlin Coding conventions
25→     * (https://kotlinlang.org/docs/coding-conventions.html) and Android's Kotlin styleguide
26→     * (https://developer.android.com/kotlin/style-guide). This codestyle also provides additional formatting on topics
27→     * which are not (explicitly) mentioned in the before mentioned styleguide. Also, this code style sometimes formats
28→     * code in a way which is not compatible with the default code formatters in IntelliJ IDEA and Android Studio. When
29→     * using this codestyle, it is best to disable (e.g. not use) automatic code formatting in the editor. Mean reason
30→     * for deviating from the code formatting provided by the editor is that those contain bugs which after some years
31→     * are still not fixed.
32→     * In the long run, this code style becomes the default code style provided by KtLint.
33→     */
34→    ktlint_official,
35→}
36→
37→public val CODE_STYLE_PROPERTY_TYPE: PropertyType.LowerCasingPropertyType<CodeStyleValue> =
38→    PropertyType.LowerCasingPropertyType(
39→        "ktlint_code_style",
40→        "The code style ('intellij_idea', 'android_studio' or 'ktlint_official') to be applied. Currently, the " +
41→            "value is defaulted to 'intellij_idea' when not set. However, in the future the default code style " +
42→            "will be changed to 'ktlint_official'.",
43→        SafeEnumValueParser(CodeStyleValue::class.java),
44→        CodeStyleValue.values().map { it.name }.toSet(),
45→    )
46→
47→public val CODE_STYLE_PROPERTY: EditorConfigProperty<CodeStyleValue> =
48→    EditorConfigProperty(
49→        type = CODE_STYLE_PROPERTY_TYPE,
50→        /*
51→         * Once the [CodeStyleValue.ktlint_official] is matured, it will become the default code style of ktlint. Until
52→         * then the [CodeStyleValue.intellij_idea] is used to remain backwards compatible.
53→         */
54→        defaultValue = CodeStyleValue.intellij_idea,
55→        androidStudioCodeStyleDefaultValue = CodeStyleValue.android_studio,
56→        intellijIdeaCodeStyleDefaultValue = CodeStyleValue.intellij_idea,
57→        ktlintOfficialCodeStyleDefaultValue = CodeStyleValue.ktlint_official,
58→    )
